name: The Wall Street Journal
philosophy: An inspired interpretation of The Wall Street Journal's design language — the American financial-newspaper of record built on a strict black-on-white broadsheet canvas, the Escrow display family (a sibling of the historic Cheltenham face the brand used pre-redesign), hedcut portraits (the dot-stippled portrait illustrations) as the brand's most-recognized graphic signature, and a 'C' market-data ticker at the masthead. The brand reads as 135-year-old American financial authority.

design_language.motion_signature: Almost no motion — 150ms ease-out on link hovers; the only signature is the masthead market-data ticker scrolling at ~30px/s and the small market-up/market-down arrow color flashes on price changes.

trademark_signals: Hedcut portraits — the dot-stippled black-and-white portrait illustrations used as journalist headshots and op-ed author marks — the brand's most-recognized graphic signature, in continuous use since 1979
Escrow display serif at every headline tier — a Hoefler-designed face descended from Cheltenham, the WSJ's historic typeface — weight 800 for hero headlines, weight 600 for body heads
Strict black-on-white broadsheet canvas — refuses any chromatic chrome except the market-data green/red and rare 'WSJ red' (#C4262E) on the brand mark moments
Market-data ticker bar at masthead — scrolling row of ticker symbols + prices + green/red arrows, the brand's persistent financial signal
Dense headline-stacking — 3–5 stacked headlines visible above the fold with byline + section eyebrow, refusing the single-hero-story pattern
ai_slop_avoided: Don't drop the hedcut portrait style for author marks. The dot-stippled portrait IS the brand's most-recognized graphic asset
Don't substitute Escrow with Times or Georgia. The Hoefler-Escrow face is the brand's typographic signature
Don't add chromatic accents outside market-up green, market-down red, and the rare WSJ red brand-mark
Don't soften the broadsheet density to a single-hero layout. The brand stacks 3-5 headlines above the fold
Don't round corners. The brand stays strictly rectangular
